channel.title: KiddieDaily — Environment
channel.description: Climate and environment news for families.

item.title: There's a Waterfall in Antarctica That Bleeds Red – Here's What's Hiding Inside It
item.description: Antarctica's Blood Falls are even weirder than they look. ScienceAlert stories are written, fact-checked, and edited by humans, never generated by AI. Don't miss a story, subscribe here.

item.title: Minute Man National Historical Park
item.description: Sites relevant to the start of the American Revolutionary War are interspersed throughout the modern-day Boston metropolitan region. The post Minute Man National Historical Park appeared first on NASA

item.title: In Germany, political fallout from heat wave begins
item.description: Following a major heat wave, critics say the government needs to do more to adapt to climate change. What can policymakers do to ensure the country is better prepared for extreme temperatures in the f

item.title: World’s oceans experience hottest June ever, scientists say more heat ahead
item.description: European Union monitors say the first half of 2026 was 'marked by sustained and exceptional ocean warmth'.

item.title: Europe's most active volcano Mount Etna erupts again
item.description: The latest eruption from Mount Etna, which is on the island of Sicily, lit up the night sky and could be seen from miles away. Find out more here.

item.title: Mexico ends a 40-year knockout drought, beats Ecuador to advance in World Cup
item.description: Mexico lost seven consecutive times at that same stage from 1994 to 2018 and didn't advance past the group stage in 2022. Now, the 40-year wait is over.

item.title: Germany saves forest from mining, US opens land to drilling
item.description: Germans have won a fight to keep a coal mine out of an ancient forest. In the US, the government is opening protected lands for drilling and development.

item.title: Blood Falls: Antarctica's Most Unsettling Waterfall Hides an Ancient World Beneath The Ice
item.description: Antarctica's Blood Falls are even weirder than they look. ScienceAlert stories are written, fact-checked, and edited by humans, never generated by AI. Don't miss a story, subscribe here.

item.title: Space Lasers Show How Venezuela’s Earthquakes Reshaped the Earth’s Crust
item.description: New satellite imagery reveals how much terrain has shifted in the wake of the twin quakes.

item.title: More Living Shorelines Could Come to States Bordering Chesapeake Bay If the Region’s Senators Get Their Way
item.description: Norfolk, Virginia, has remained the poster child for cities facing land subsidence and flooding in the country, struggling through an onslaught of storm surges and blue-sky flooding that become more f

item.title: Spain heat wave kills over 1,000 in second-hottest June ever
item.description: At least 1,028 people died during Spain's recent heatwave as the country endured its hottest January-to-June period on record. Scientists said the extreme heat across Europe was driven by climate chan

item.title: Powerful X-class solar flare triggers radio blackouts across North America
item.description: A coronal mass ejection accompanied the X1.1 solar flare.

item.title: Wildlife’s unpredictable movements make climate-change planning difficult
item.description: New England’s winters have long been a punishing force. Forests fade in and out of deep freezes. Animals pile on fat for warmth in anticipation of the harsh weather to come. Others flee south, seeking

item.title: Nepal’s new government bets on tax revenue over clean energy push
item.description: KATHMANDU — When Balendra Shah took office as Nepal’s new prime minister in March following a landslide victory for his party, he inherited a fuel crisis triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran. His

item.title: Slowdown of AMOC ocean current may be gradual and reversible
item.description: Scientists worry that a surge of meltwater from Greenland could irreversibly collapse the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation, but new modelling suggests the weakening of the current could be

item.title: In 1776, the solar system only had 6 planets. Now, it has 8. Does it end there?
item.description: As the United States prepares to celebrate its 250th anniversary, the history of the solar system tells its own story of exploration.

item.title: Antarctica's Blood Falls Hides a Hidden World That's Never Seen The Sun
item.description: Antarctica's Blood Falls are even weirder than they look. ScienceAlert stories are written, fact-checked, and edited by humans, never generated by AI. Don't miss a story, subscribe here.

item.title: Two Scientists Found a Bone in Antarctica in 1985. It Took 41 Years to Realize What They'd Actually Discovered
item.description: We're kind of glad scientists are hoarders. ScienceAlert stories are written, fact-checked, and edited by humans, never generated by AI. Don't miss a story, subscribe here.

item.title: Venezuela's interim regime is using the earthquakes to bury democracy
item.description: Chavista military forces did not arrive at the disaster zone with shovels and pickaxes, but with pistols and rifles.

item.title: Ocean-monitoring satellite spots wildfire smoke from space | Space photo of the day for July 1, 2026
item.description: A NASA satellite has found an unintended, important purpose.
